jokes.text: - Oh! - Oh! Oh!
jokes.setup: Workers are excited that Mr. Burns is finally leaving so they can go home
jokes.punchline: Their excitement turns to dismay when he starts talking to someone else
jokes.explanation: Basic reaction comedy with minimal setup, relies on simple workplace frustration

jokes.text: Listen carefully. I have taken your wife hostage. If you don't have a wife, I have kidnapped your brother. Nod if you understand. Now back away from Burns and I will let your dog live.
jokes.setup: Homer needs to get someone away from Mr. Burns
jokes.punchline: He creates an elaborate fake hostage scenario with escalating threats covering all possible family members
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Homer's absurd problem-solving with escalating specificity and character-driven logic

jokes.text: Now stop and dance like a happy prospector. Happier. Happier!
jokes.setup: Homer is directing someone in his fake hostage scenario
jokes.punchline: The demands become increasingly specific and ridiculous, demanding happier dancing
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up to the hostage bit with escalating absurdity and visual comedy

jokes.text: Homer, we want to get him away from Burns. Oh, right. Just leave. But kick like a Rockette.
jokes.setup: Someone points out the obvious solution to Homer's elaborate scheme
jokes.punchline: Homer accepts the simple solution but still adds an absurd dancing requirement
jokes.explanation: Shows Homer's inability to abandon theatrical elements even when taking the simple path

jokes.text: We're free! Empty apartment here I come! Set the table, Marge. I can already taste those deep-fried pork chops.
jokes.setup: Homer is excited to be free from work and go home
jokes.punchline: He immediately puts Marge to work and mentions unhealthy food, showing his selfishness
jokes.explanation: Standard Homer selfishness with good character consistency but predictable

jokes.text: When you ask me that it's never good.
jokes.setup: Marge asks if Homer remembers what today is
jokes.punchline: Homer's meta-awareness that this question always leads to bad news for him
jokes.explanation: Great meta-humor about sitcom spouse dynamics, highly relatable and economical

jokes.text: Bart says that we got a call from NASA, and your gravity is pulling satellites out of their orbits.
jokes.setup: Marge is worried about Homer's weight
jokes.punchline: She cites an impossibly exaggerated consequence involving NASA and orbital mechanics
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation of fat jokes into scientific absurdity, highly quotable and original

jokes.text: Marge, that was a joke. But it comes from a true place.
jokes.setup: Marge took Bart's exaggerated joke seriously
jokes.punchline: Homer's pseudo-philosophical analysis of joke construction while missing the point
jokes.explanation: Excellent meta-comedy about emotional truth in humor, delivered with perfect Homer obliviousness

jokes.text: I think I'll miss you most of all, Captain Corn Dog Schnitzel Palace.
jokes.setup: Homer is saying goodbye to his favorite unhealthy foods
jokes.punchline: The restaurant name is an absurd combination of different ethnic foods and Dorothy's line from Wizard of Oz
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist restaurant name with cultural reference, though the Wizard of Oz callback is obvious

jokes.text: Can't let Marge see this.
jokes.setup: Homer is doing something he shouldn't while supposedly starting his diet
jokes.punchline: His immediate guilt and secrecy about whatever food transgression he just committed
jokes.explanation: Standard Homer sneaking food bit, predictable but character-consistent

jokes.text: A drive-up trash can. This must be how the rich toss out their gold.
jokes.setup: Homer encounters a convenience he's never seen before
jokes.punchline: He attributes it to wealth and imagines rich people throwing away gold
jokes.explanation: Good Homer logic connecting convenience to wealth with absurd conclusion

jokes.text: Hmm, 'Dispose of properly.' Aw, this book is too hard.
jokes.setup: Homer finds something that needs proper disposal
jokes.punchline: He treats a simple disposal instruction like it's incomprehensible literature
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer anti-intellectualism, treating basic instructions as advanced reading

jokes.text: There, there, Cheesy McMayor. No one likes weepy meat.
jokes.setup: Mayor is comforting a crying fast food mascot
jokes.punchline: The absurd name 'Cheesy McMayor' and the phrase 'weepy meat' treating a mascot as actual food
jokes.explanation: Great absurdist mascot name and the concept of anthropomorphic food having emotions

jokes.text: I don't want to live in a future where food is brought by 'waiters'... where the chairs aren't attached to the tables... and where I can't ditch my kids in a pit of dirty balls.
jokes.setup: Mayor is making an impassioned speech about rebuilding fast food
jokes.punchline: He lists increasingly absurd 'horrors' of sit-down restaurants, ending with abandoning children
jokes.explanation: Brilliant satire of fast food culture with perfect escalation to the ball pit punchline

jokes.text: But won't that just shift the burden to your children? No, you idiot. We just pay for it with another bond issue. Let her figure out someone to dump it on.
jokes.setup: Citizen raises legitimate concern about fiscal responsibility
jokes.punchline: Mayor's callous dismissal and plan to keep pushing debt to future generations
jokes.explanation: Sharp political satire about debt shifting with good cynical politician characterization

jokes.text: Uh-oh, they're going to be cheesed.
jokes.setup: Springfield moves their primary ahead of New Hampshire's
jokes.punchline: Massive understatement using 'cheesed' for what would be political fury
jokes.explanation: Mild wordplay and understatement, functional but not particularly clever

jokes.text: Providing cheap liquor to Massachusetts teens for 200 years.
jokes.setup: Description of New Hampshire appears on screen
jokes.punchline: Instead of typical state motto, it's about their role in underage drinking
jokes.explanation: Good regional humor about New Hampshire's liquor laws and border state dynamics

jokes.text: this race is as wide open as a hobo's mouth at a pie-flinging contest.
jokes.setup: Dan Rather is describing the presidential race
jokes.punchline: His signature bizarre folksy metaphor comparing politics to hobo pie-eating
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dan Rather parody with his characteristic absurd down-home metaphors

jokes.text: Well, Mr. Rather, the way I see it, as my father always said, 'The way I look at it...'
jokes.setup: Reporter asks for opinion on candidates
jokes.punchline: Circular non-answer that starts a quote but just repeats the same phrase
jokes.explanation: Good satire of meaningless political soundbites and rural interview clichés

jokes.text: Grandma was slow, but she was old. You have no excuse. Into the truck!
jokes.setup: Rather is rushing his news crew to Springfield
jokes.punchline: His harsh motivational speech comparing the crew unfavorably to a dead grandmother
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor showing Rather's intensity with perfectly economical harsh logic

jokes.text: Which Springfield? The one the Simpsons live in.
jokes.setup: Driver asks which Springfield to go to
jokes.punchline: Meta-reference acknowledging the show's deliberately ambiguous location
jokes.explanation: Smart meta-joke about the show's famous geographic ambiguity, breaking the fourth wall cleverly

jokes.text: Nice knitting on these masks, Martha. Now that you've said my name I have to kill him.
jokes.setup: Bank robbers are complimenting each other during a heist
jokes.punchline: The polite domestic compliment followed by matter-of-fact murder logic
jokes.explanation: Great juxtaposition of domestic pleasantries with criminal violence, perfectly absurd logic

jokes.text: our humble city is overrun with candidates, newshounds, spin doctors, hacks, flacks, Russerts, Blitzers and even the occasional voter.
jokes.setup: Kent is describing the media circus in Springfield
jokes.punchline: Using actual journalists' names as job categories and treating voters as the rarest species
jokes.explanation: Sharp media satire using real journalists' names as generic terms and prioritizing media over voters

jokes.text: Yeah, I like girls, fruit loop.
jokes.setup: Reporter asks if he has a preference (meaning political)
jokes.punchline: Barney misunderstands and answers about sexual preference, adding an insult
jokes.explanation: Standard misunderstanding joke but fits Barney's crude character well

jokes.text: I'm a registered... something.
jokes.setup: Reporter asks if he's a registered voter
jokes.punchline: Barney's uncertainty about what he's registered for, implying criminal record
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Barney's legal troubles and general confusion

jokes.text: That's what you said about yellow fever, and that was no fun.
jokes.setup: Homer says to 'catch' primary fever
jokes.punchline: Marge references a previous time Homer used the same phrase about an actual deadly disease
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback showing Marge's memory and Homer's pattern of inappropriate enthusiasm

jokes.text: I feel like a pundit in a think tank! Think tank, eh? Hmm. Now let's consider World Bank lending policy towards Micronesia.
jokes.setup: Lisa feels smart about politics, Homer hears 'think tank'
jokes.punchline: Homer immediately tries to sound intellectual with an absurdly specific policy topic
jokes.explanation: Great Homer moment showing his fake intellectualism with hilariously specific but random policy knowledge

jokes.text: What? I'm not allowed to get one right?
jokes.setup: Homer surprises everyone by knowing something intellectual
jokes.punchline: His defensive reaction to being right for once, breaking character awareness
jokes.explanation: Nice meta moment where Homer acknowledges his own character limitations with wounded pride

jokes.text: Sure thing, mouse pad. Ten bucks.
jokes.setup: Reporter asks about internet access at Moe's bar
jokes.punchline: Moe's casual insult 'mouse pad' and his immediate price gouging
jokes.explanation: Good Moe characterization with his typical rudeness and opportunistic pricing

jokes.text: Do I need to say my name? People know me, right? Know it's just cable, but... You know what? I'm not gonna worry about it. I gonna just... Great. Now I'm worried.
jokes.setup: Jon Stewart is doing a report from Springfield
jokes.punchline: His neurotic spiral about his fame level and cable vs. network status
jokes.explanation: Good Jon Stewart parody capturing his neurotic, self-deprecating personality accurately

jokes.text: Haven't seen you since you bailed on that benefit. Yeah, well, I really didn't believe in the cause. Well, Krusty's Kids sure missed ya. Yeah, they're great. A little clingy...
jokes.setup: Stewart mentions Krusty bailing on a charity benefit
jokes.punchline: Krusty's honest admission he didn't believe in the cause, then calling sick kids 'clingy'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Krusty characterization showing his selfishness and inappropriate attitude toward charity

jokes.text: Are you writing this down? Nah, this is somethin' else.
jokes.setup: Stewart catches Krusty writing while they talk
jokes.punchline: Krusty's obvious lie about not stealing jokes, while clearly doing exactly that
jokes.explanation: Great comedy industry satire about joke stealing with perfect Krusty shamelessness

jokes.text: Rush Limbaugh's sweatpants?
jokes.setup: Krusty is fishing for Stewart's 'hot air' joke completion
jokes.punchline: His gross-out suggestion about Rush Limbaugh's clothing
jokes.explanation: Cheap gross-out humor relying on fat jokes and political figure mockery without much craft

jokes.text: Well, I'll thank you to keep my zingers out of your mouth. I'm gonna try and remember you the way you used to be. But I've always been terrible!
jokes.setup: Stewart tries to end the conversation nobly
jokes.punchline: Krusty's self-aware admission that he's always been bad, undermining the sentiment
jokes.explanation: Perfect Krusty self-awareness subverting Stewart's attempt at dramatic closure

jokes.text: Your medium is dying! Nelson! But it is! There's being right, and there's being nice.
jokes.setup: Nelson mocks a print journalist about newspapers dying
jokes.punchline: When scolded, he points out he's technically correct, gets lesson about tact
jokes.explanation: Good media industry satire delivered through Nelson's characteristic bluntness and cruelty

jokes.text: This punch is too sweet. your blouse makes you look fat. Opinions about the ads.
jokes.setup: Moderator asks for honest opinions, gets personal attacks instead
jokes.punchline: Has to redirect back to the actual topic after brutal honesty about wrong things
jokes.explanation: Decent misdirection about 'honest opinions' but fairly predictable focus group humor

jokes.text: Mm, my god! They're going steady!
jokes.setup: Political ad shows candidate and terrorist leader in same newspaper
jokes.punchline: Participant interprets it as a romantic relationship rather than guilt by association
jokes.explanation: Clever misinterpretation of political attack ad logic, showing voter naivety humorously

jokes.text: Well, I thought every part of it was good, but overall, I hated it. I feel exactly the opposite, but the same.
jokes.setup: Focus group giving opinions on political ad
jokes.punchline: Completely contradictory and nonsensical opinions that make no logical sense
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist take on focus group meaninglessness but somewhat forced contradiction

jokes.text: That was the best milkshake ad I've ever seen. It makes me wanna want a milkshake!
jokes.setup: Participant commenting on political attack ad
jokes.punchline: Completely missed the point and thought it was advertising milkshakes
jokes.explanation: Good voter confusion joke showing complete misunderstanding of political messaging

jokes.text: Hence my campaign slogan, 'End quintile disparity'. Look, we all care about quintile disparity, but the Democrats can't win with another prissy brainiac.
jokes.setup: Candidate gives wonky economic speech about inequality
jokes.punchline: Another candidate points out this intellectual approach loses elections
jokes.explanation: Smart political satire about intellectual candidates being unelectable but somewhat inside-baseball

jokes.text: That's Whiner-girl.
jokes.setup: Someone mispronounces the senator's name as 'Winnergill'
jokes.punchline: Correction reveals the name is actually 'Whiner-girl', which is even worse
jokes.explanation: Decent wordplay on politician names but fairly straightforward pun without much depth

jokes.text: You're never gonna let that go, are you?
jokes.setup: Marge is making Ned put up campaign signs as apparent punishment
jokes.punchline: Ned's resigned acceptance suggests this is payback for some past transgression
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing marital dynamics and Ned's guilt, implies backstory

jokes.text: Family values! Vanishing middle class! Cut and run! Terrorists win! Terrorists win!
jokes.setup: Politicians are shouting competing slogans at the Simpson house
jokes.punchline: The slogans devolve into meaningless repeated buzzwords
jokes.explanation: Good satire of empty political rhetoric and the repetition of fear-based messaging

jokes.text: If you haven't sprung from her or aren't married to my loins, get the hell out of this house!
jokes.setup: Homer is kicking out the politicians
jokes.punchline: His crude but specific way of defining family relationships using 'loins'
jokes.explanation: Great Homer line combining his crudeness with oddly precise biological terminology

jokes.text: You too, Fred Thompson. But I was in Die Hard. Die Hard Two!
jokes.setup: Homer is kicking everyone out including Fred Thompson
jokes.punchline: Thompson's desperate appeal to his movie career, correcting which Die Hard he was in
jokes.explanation: Good celebrity politician joke about Thompson's desperation and pride in mediocre roles

jokes.text: I could really go for some kind of military dictator like, uh, Juan Peron. When he disappeared ya, you stayed disappeared. Plus his wife was Madonna.
jokes.setup: Moe suggests wanting a military dictator
jokes.punchline: Casual endorsement of murder and disappearances, plus confusing Eva Perón with Madonna
jokes.explanation: Perfect Moe moment combining casual fascism with pop culture confusion, darkly funny

jokes.text: Why don't we all pick the most ridiculous candidate and write him in? You mean Dennis Kucinich? Hey! I'm right here!
jokes.setup: Citizens want to write in the most ridiculous candidate
jokes.punchline: Someone suggests Kucinich, who's actually present and offended
jokes.explanation: Good political humor about Kucinich's reputation with perfect timing of his presence

jokes.text: What I did made the TV thing happen.
jokes.setup: Ralph sees himself on TV as a presidential candidate
jokes.punchline: His childish inability to understand cause and effect or describe events properly
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ralph dialogue showing his limited comprehension and unique speech patterns

jokes.text: Two days ago, this bed-wetter made a splash in the Springfield Primary.
jokes.setup: News anchor describes Ralph's political success
jokes.punchline: Using 'bed-wetter' and 'made a splash' together for double meaning
jokes.explanation: Decent pun playing on water/urine imagery but fairly obvious wordplay

jokes.text: Stranger danger!
jokes.setup: Ralph's position on immigration reform
jokes.punchline: He responds with a child safety slogan instead of policy
jokes.explanation: Brilliant political satire showing how child-like fear translates to adult policy, perfectly economic

jokes.text: I only have this much monies.
jokes.setup: Ralph's stance on government spending
jokes.punchline: His childish grammar and presumably small gesture about his limited money
jokes.explanation: Great Ralph moment showing childish understanding of economics with perfect grammar mistakes

jokes.text: Well, um, I'd hate to be the fingernail in this Cobb salad, but we don't even know if this young man is a Democrat or Republican.
jokes.setup: Pundit wants to raise a concern about Ralph
jokes.punchline: Uses completely nonsensical food metaphor instead of standard expressions
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist pundit speak with creative food metaphor, though somewhat forced

jokes.text: Before we invite Ralph to the prom, we should find out if he's wearing a tux or a tutu.
jokes.setup: Continuing the concern about Ralph's unknown party affiliation
jokes.punchline: Using prom metaphor with tux/tutu as Democrat/Republican symbols
jokes.explanation: Decent extended metaphor about political uncertainty with gender-based party imagery

jokes.text: Ralph said he'd let me be Secretary of Indian burns. Here's my first official act: Ow!
jokes.setup: Bart supports Ralph and mentions a cabinet position
jokes.punchline: He immediately demonstrates the position by giving someone an Indian burn
jokes.explanation: Good Bart moment combining his mischievousness with creative interpretation of government roles

jokes.text: Lisa, being president is easy. You just point the Army and shoot.
jokes.setup: Lisa objects that Ralph is too young to be president
jokes.punchline: Homer's casual, violent oversimplification of presidential duties
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment showing his simplistic worldview and casual attitude toward violence

jokes.text: The Constitution? I'm pretty sure the Patriot Act killed it to ensure our freedoms.
jokes.setup: Lisa mentions constitutional age requirements
jokes.punchline: Homer's confused understanding of how the Patriot Act works, with unintentional irony
jokes.explanation: Excellent political satire about civil liberties with perfect Homer logic and unintentional irony

jokes.text: Oh, the Patriot Act is so terrible! The government might find out what library books I take out. What's next? Finding out what operas I go to?
jokes.setup: Marge sarcastically defends the Patriot Act
jokes.punchline: Her examples are hilariously innocent and reveal her wholesome lifestyle
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge characterization showing her naivety and wholesome concerns about privacy

jokes.text: Do you have bats in your belfry? They don't even let him use big-boy scissors!
jokes.setup: Republicans are considering nominating Ralph
jokes.punchline: Official points out Ralph's childish limitations with specific example
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing the absurdity of nominating a child with specific safety concern

jokes.text: Sure he's a little green, but, uh, you know, so was George W. Bush, and look how great, uh, he-he... um...
jokes.setup: Republican tries to defend Ralph's inexperience
jokes.punchline: Can't finish the comparison to Bush because he realizes it proves the opposite point
jokes.explanation: Good political humor about Bush's presidency with perfect comedic timing on the failed defense

jokes.text: But at least he won! The second time... assuming they don't find those ballot boxes in Ohio.
jokes.setup: Official tries to salvage the Bush comparison
jokes.punchline: References election fraud conspiracy theories, undermining his own argument
jokes.explanation: Sharp political humor about election controversies with good self-defeating logic

jokes.text: I already got my assless, sparkle tux cleaned for the inauguration.
jokes.setup: Democrat is excited about Ralph's nomination
jokes.punchline: Describes an absurdly inappropriate formal wear choice for inauguration
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist humor about inappropriate formal wear, though relies on shock value

jokes.text: With Ralph on the ticket, I don't know how we will blow it, but we will. Because that's what the Democratic Party is all about.
jokes.setup: Democrat discusses Ralph's electability
jokes.punchline: Brutally honest assessment of Democratic Party's ability to snatch defeat from victory
jokes.explanation: Excellent political satire about Democratic Party incompetence with perfect self-awareness

jokes.text: the most charismatic child since Drew Barrymore in Firestarter. When she got mad, they got burned.
jokes.setup: Kent compares Ralph to charismatic children
jokes.punchline: Chooses a horror movie child character and explains the obvious pun
jokes.explanation: Weak pun that explains itself, relying on obvious movie reference without much craft

jokes.text: Elmo?
jokes.setup: Asked to choose between Republican elephant and Democratic donkey
jokes.punchline: Ralph chooses a completely different red character from Sesame Street
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ralph moment showing his childish associations and complete political obliviousness

jokes.text: Ah, Admiral Elmo Zumwalt, Richard Nixon's chief of naval operations. So Ralph's a Republican!
jokes.setup: Ralph says 'Elmo' when asked about party preference
jokes.punchline: Operative connects it to an obscure military official, reaching for political meaning
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist humor showing how political operatives read meaning into anything

jokes.text: Only one place to settle this: to the offices of the World Book Encyclopedia!
jokes.setup: Heated debate over naval history needs resolution
jokes.punchline: Treating encyclopedia offices like a dramatic destination for fact-checking
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist escalation treating reference material research as high adventure

jokes.text: We have Alec Baldwin, they have Stephen Baldwin. They might as well not even have a Baldwin.
jokes.setup: Democrat tries to convince Ralph to join their party
jokes.punchline: Uses celebrity endorsements as selling point, ranking the Baldwin brothers
jokes.explanation: Good celebrity culture humor about the relative value of different Baldwin brothers

jokes.text: Maybe I want to use them. Use them to make this country great again. When we're mad, we'll just use our words, then the rest of the world will play nice with us. And the only boom-booms will be in our pants.
jokes.setup: Lisa says politicians want to use Ralph
jokes.punchline: Ralph's childish but surprisingly wise foreign policy using playground logic
jokes.explanation: Brilliant moment where Ralph's childish language actually contains profound wisdom about diplomacy

jokes.text: And you can be my first ladle.
jokes.setup: Ralph is offering Lisa a position in his administration
jokes.punchline: His mispronunciation of 'First Lady' as 'first ladle' (kitchen utensil)
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ralph wordplay showing his innocent confusion while being accidentally sweet

jokes.text: I want a tricycle, and a dog who won't chew my Hot Wheels, and a brighter future for America! I'm Ralph Wiggum, and I've been a good boy!
jokes.setup: Ralph's closing political ad statement
jokes.punchline: Mixes childish personal wants with presidential promises, ends with behavior report
jokes.explanation: Perfect finale showing Ralph's innocent mixing of childhood desires with presidential campaign rhetoric
